title_en: Commentaries and Lasting Influence on Muslim Devotional Life
title_ar: الشروح والأثر الدائم على الحياة الروحية للمسلمين
content_en:
Al-Adhkar has generated a rich commentarial tradition reflecting its importance in the Islamic educational and devotional heritage. The most celebrated classical commentary is Fath al-Ilah al-Malik fi Hall Alfaz al-Adhkar (Opening the Dominion of the Sovereign in Explaining the Expressions of Al-Adhkar) by the Moroccan scholar Ibn Allan as-Siddiq, whose multi-volume work analyzes each remembrance formula in Al-Adhkar with close attention to its chain of transmission, its linguistic content, and its legal and devotional implications. Ibn Allan's commentary remains a standard reference for scholars seeking deeper engagement with al-Adhkar's content.

The influence of Al-Adhkar on subsequent compilations of adhkar is vast. Nearly every subsequent collection of Muslim supplications and remembrances — from the Ottoman period to the present — reflects the organization and content of an-Nawawi's original compilation. The most widely distributed contemporary adhkar booklet, Hisn al-Muslim by Said al-Qahtani, draws on Al-Adhkar as one of its primary sources, and the popularity of Hisn al-Muslim across the Muslim world is itself testimony to the enduring relevance of the material that an-Nawawi organized and preserved.

Al-Albani's critical engagement with Al-Adhkar, in which he assessed the authentication of individual traditions and noted the weak ones, has introduced a degree of hadith critical scrutiny into popular engagement with the book. His notes on weak traditions in Al-Adhkar have been influential among communities that follow his approach to hadith, while scholars in other traditions continue to follow the classical principle of using weaker traditions for devotional encouragement without legal implications.

For contemporary Muslim educators and spiritual guides, Al-Adhkar remains one of the most practically important texts in the Islamic tradition. Its comprehensive, occasion-organized collection of authenticated remembrances makes it a complete guide to the prophetically grounded devotional life. Teaching students to use Al-Adhkar — to internalize its remembrances and to make them a living part of daily practice — has been a goal of Islamic spiritual education from the time of an-Nawawi to the present day.
